summary:
Claim: The palatal plane is posteriorly downward in long face syndrome due to increased lower facial height.  
Evidence: - Increased lower facial height correlates with maxillary retrusion and posterior displacement of the palatal plane. - Cone-beam CT studies show reduced anteroposterior depth of the maxillary cavity, confirming posterior positioning of the palatal plane.  
Answer: A  

This summary maintains core clinical logic by linking observed morphological changes—specifically elongation of the lower face—to structural shifts in the palate. It integrates both traditional developmental patterns and modern imaging data to validate the conclusion. The claim reflects well-established craniofacial biomechanics, where excessive vertical growth in the lower face results in relative backward movement of the maxilla and its associated structures. Key anatomical relationships are preserved without oversimplification or speculative assumptions. The evidence supports this through direct correlation and empirical validation via advanced imaging techniques. Together, these points establish a robust rationale for selecting option A over alternatives suggesting anterior or horizontal shifts. No contradictory factors are introduced; instead, all elements reinforce consistency across historical observations and current research.
